title: Business Review
source: Starter KB grounded in widely used strategy and goal-setting frameworks and UX strategy guidance. Key references: Google re:Work OKRs guide; NN/g UX strategy and UX roadmaps; HBR strategy frameworks (e.g., Porter Five Forces; SWOT usage guidance).
purpose: Provide reference knowledge for capturing, reviewing, and applying business context to product and design work: organizational intent (why), goals (OKRs), measures (KPIs), plans (roadmaps), and decision governance. Designed so teams can validate design decisions against strategy and avoid context switching.
scope: Applies to product/business context used to guide design and development: why statement/vision, strategy pillars, OKRs, KPIs, initiatives, roadmap, risks, assumptions, and decision history. This starter KB defines structure and best practices. It does not contain any org-specific confidential content; those should be added later via private KBs.
definitions: ### Why Statement (Purpose)
A concise statement of the organization's reason for existing and the human or customer value it aims to create (distinct from revenue goals).
### Vision
A description of the desired future state the organization is working toward; long-term and directional.
### Strategy
A set of choices about where to play and how to win, expressed as priorities and tradeoffs, not a list of projects.
### Strategy Pillar
A major theme that focuses investment and clarifies priorities (e.g., “Trust & reliability”, “Self-serve growth”).
### Objective (OKR)
A qualitative, outcome-oriented goal describing what impact is desired (not a task list). (Google re:Work OKRs) (https://rework.withgoogle.com/intl/en/guides/set-goals-with-okrs/)
### Key Result (OKR)
A measurable outcome that indicates progress toward an objective; should be specific and verifiable.
### KPI
A metric used to measure performance over time (health of a product or business outcome). KPIs can inform OKRs but are not the same as OKRs.
### Initiative
A coordinated set of work that contributes to one or more OKRs; initiatives are how goals are achieved (projects, programs, releases).
### North Star Metric
A primary metric that best represents the value delivered to customers (context-dependent); used to align teams on value creation.
### Assumption
A belief taken as true for planning that should be validated (e.g., user behavior, market conditions).
### Risk
A potential event or condition that could harm outcomes (delivery, adoption, compliance, trust).
### Decision Log
A record of key decisions, including context, alternatives, and rationale, to prevent re-litigating and to preserve institutional memory.
rulesConstraints: Treat OKRs as outcomes, not task lists; define impact first and allow teams to choose methods. (Google re:Work OKRs) (https://rework.withgoogle.com/intl/en/guides/set-goals-with-okrs/)
Each Objective should have 2-5 Key Results; each Key Result must be measurable and time-bound.
Key Results should measure outcomes (behavior, success, quality), not outputs (shipping features) unless an output is the only verifiable proxy.
Avoid metric overload: identify a small set of decision-driving KPIs and clearly define each metric (formula, data source, cadence).
Always capture tradeoffs: what will not be done, what will be deprioritized, and why.
Tie initiatives and roadmap items to objectives and key results; if work cannot be traced to strategy or operational necessity, challenge it.
Document assumptions explicitly and define validation plans (signals, experiments, research) before large investments.
Maintain a decision log for major product/design decisions: context, options considered, decision, and rationale.
Keep strategy artifacts current: include dates, owners, and review cadence; stale strategy is worse than no strategy.
Privacy and confidentiality: do not store sensitive customer or employee personal data in public/shared business-review KBs; use redaction and private KBs where needed.

do: Write a crisp Why/Vision statement that is stable and can be used to evaluate decisions.
Define 3-6 strategy pillars that clearly guide prioritization and tradeoffs.

Use OKRs to align on outcomes and focus; use initiatives/roadmap to describe the work planned to pursue those outcomes. (Google re:Work OKRs) (https://rework.withgoogle.com/intl/en/guides/set-goals-with-okrs/)
Define each KPI with a one-line meaning, formula, data source, and owner to prevent metric confusion.
Create a simple mapping: Pillar → Objective → Key Results → Initiatives → UX/Engineering deliverables.
Maintain an assumptions list and validate the highest-risk assumptions early (research, prototype tests, experiments).
Maintain a decision log to reduce churn and preserve rationale; include links to evidence when available.
Include leading indicators (early signals) and lagging indicators (business outcomes) in measurement plans.
Use a regular review cadence (monthly/quarterly) to track OKR progress and update priorities.
dont: Don't treat OKRs as a backlog or checklist of tasks. (Google re:Work OKRs) (https://rework.withgoogle.com/intl/en/guides/set-goals-with-okrs/)
Don't use vague objectives (e.g., “Improve UX”) without defining what improvement means and how it will be measured.
Don't optimize for one metric at the expense of trust, quality, or accessibility; document counter-metrics when needed.
Don't ship initiatives without a clear link to strategy or to operational requirements (security, compliance, reliability).
Don't overwrite or delete history; maintain versioning (updatedAt) and keep prior decisions accessible.
Don't mix confidential org strategy into a public starter KB; keep org-specific content in private KBs with governance.

examples: “Help people accomplish complex work with clarity, confidence, and control.”
Pillar 1: Time-to-value, Pillar 2: Trust & reliability, Pillar 3: Collaboration, Pillar 4: Self-serve scalability.

Objective: “Reduce friction for first-time users completing their first project.” Key Results: “Increase first-project completion rate from 35% to 50%”; “Reduce median time-to-first-success from 12 min to 7 min”; “Reduce onboarding-related support contacts by 20%.” (OKRs as outcomes) (https://rework.withgoogle.com/intl/en/guides/set-goals-with-okrs/)
Activation rate = % of new users who complete the first success action within 7 days. Source: event analytics. Cadence: weekly. Owner: Growth PM.

Decision: Use a multi-step onboarding flow rather than a single long form. Rationale: reduces cognitive load; supports progressive disclosure; aligns with time-to-value pillar. Evidence: usability sessions (n=8) showed fewer completion errors and higher confidence.
edgeCases: Conflicting goals: when an OKR conflicts with a quality/safety constraint (e.g., growth vs trust), define explicit counter-metrics and guardrails.
Ambiguous ownership: if a KPI or OKR has no clear owner, it will drift; assign owners and review cadence before relying on it.
Data gaps: if metrics are not instrumented or are unreliable, define temporary proxies and an instrumentation initiative; document uncertainty.
Multi-product organizations: use a top-level strategy + product-level OKRs; avoid forcing one North Star across unrelated products.
Volatile markets: treat competitive/trend insights as time-bound; include updatedAt and a refresh cadence; document assumptions that may change.
Regulated environments: incorporate compliance, accessibility, and security as non-negotiable constraints; reflect them in decision logs and counter-metrics.
Strategy churn: if leadership changes direction frequently, preserve history via decision logs and explicitly note what changed and why.
